title: Marble cake
subtitle: Swirled vanilla and chocolate butter cake
excerpt: A classic butter cake with a vanilla sponge and dark cocoa swirl. One batter, split in two, layered and swirled in a loaf tin. Simple to make, striking to slice.
sourceNotes: The marble cake (Marmorkuchen) is of German origin, documented in nineteenth-century domestic cookery. The British version follows the same principle: a plain butter cake split in two, one portion mixed with cocoa, then layered and swirled before baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Wrap slices individually. Freeze for up to 2 months.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps well for 4 days in a tin.

glossaryTerms.definition: Creating a two-colour swirl in a cake by spooning two differently flavoured batters into a tin alternately, then drawing a skewer through both in a figure-of-eight. A few passes creates distinct swirls; too many and the batters blend into a uniform grey.

content.text: Marble cake is one batter made in two colours. The technique requires only restraint: a few strokes of a skewer is enough. Stop too soon and the pattern looks underworked; keep going and the colours blend together. Three or four figure-of-eights is the right amount.
Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Grease a loaf tin and line with baking paper.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} and {{caster-sugar}} together until pale and fluffy, about 4 minutes. Beat in the {{free-range-eggs}} one at a time, then the {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{fine-sea-salt}} in two additions, alternating with the {{whole-milk}}.
Transfer half the batter to a second bowl. Sift the {{cocoa-powder}} over one bowl and fold it in. Spoon the two batters into the tin in alternating dollops. Then
by drawing a skewer through the batters in a figure-of-eight three or four times.
Bake for 50–55 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.
